(a) = appetizer attachment rate (share of checks adding an appetizer). (b) = generic 'attachment rate' above 2019 levels — likely the same appetizer/add-on attachment metric, but the (b) evidence does not specify what item is being attached, and (b) carries an unrelated 'guest_incident_rate' variant. Scope of (b) is ambiguous, so cannot confirm same object.

Different objects/scope. (a) is the price premium charged on DELIVERY/third-party channel menus vs in-restaurant (~2-3% markup). (b) is general menu price increases at the North Italia brand restaurants. Different mechanism (channel pricing markup vs brand-level menu price hikes) and different scope (delivery channel vs one specific brand).

Different mechanism/object. (a) is the competitive/marketing use of social media to differentiate brand messaging (offensive marketing capability). (b) is the reputational risk of negative publicity going viral on social media (defensive reputation harm). Same medium but opposite directions: marketing advantage vs viral-reputation damage.

Different financial instruments. (a) is incurring debt / direct financial obligations (8-K Item 2.03 borrowing). (b) is issuing equity/common stock and its dilutive effect on share price. Debt financing vs equity financing are distinct objects and mechanisms.

Different scope and mechanism. (a) is shifts in consumer DINING/food preferences affecting market share and menu relevance. (b) evidence is specifically about changing consumer preferences and DEMOGRAPHICS in a given area causing the company to relocate or terminate restaurant LEASES — a real-estate/location-driven mechanism, not menu/dining-taste. Different downstream mechanism and scope despite overlapping wording.

Different scope. (a) describes the bakery division's total output producing for the company's OWN restaurants, international licensees AND third-party customers (broad division). (b) is the specific reported financial metric 'external bakery sales' — only the third-party-customer revenue line (~$12-21M quarterly). (b) is a subset/specific metric, not the same scope as the whole division.

Different objects. (a) is the buyback program itself (capital return, dilution offset, dollars of stock repurchased). (b) is the 1% Inflation Reduction Act EXCISE TAX cost imposed on repurchases. One is the capital-allocation action; the other is a tax-cost driver on that action. Different metric and mechanism.

Different mechanism. (a) is the seasonal sales LIFT from holidays (e.g., North Italia drives strong volumes in holiday season — a demand pattern). (b) is the calendar-SHIFT/timing effect causing year-over-year comparison distortions (holiday falling in a different quarter, basis-point comp impacts). Seasonal demand strength vs period-comparison shift are distinct mechanisms.

Unrelated — only share the token 'credit'. (a) is revolver/borrowing availability (liquidity from a credit facility). (b) is the FICA tip TAX CREDIT / SITCA program tax benefit. Completely different objects and mechanisms (financing liquidity vs payroll-tax credit).

Opposite scopes. (a) is high-income/$100K+ consumer spending behavior (the company's core cohort, resilient). (b) is low-end/lower-income consumer spending pressure. Different consumer segments with opposite dynamics — not the same object.

Different scope. (a) is average weekly/per-operating-week sales for the core Cheesecake Factory restaurants (~$239K/wk). (b) is the same metric type but scoped to the FLOWER CHILD brand specifically (~$85K/wk). Same metric, different brand scope — a brand-specific subset, not the same object.

Food cost = cost of input ingredients/COGS; menu pricing = price increases charged to customers. Different metric and opposite side of the P&L. Mechanism differs (cost reduction via best-practices vs revenue from price hikes).
AUV = average sales per unit in dollars ($8.0M, $7.7M); segment margin = profitability percentage of the segment. Different metric (top-line volume vs profitability ratio).
Acquisition = bringing Flower Child fully under ownership (M&A/integration event); margin = hitting a mid-teens profitability target. Different object and mechanism.
AUV = absolute dollar sales per box ($4.4M run-rate); same-store/comparable sales = year-over-year growth percentage of existing units. Different metric (level vs growth rate).

Delivery cost = rising expense of third-party delivery services; vendor failure = operational/security failure of a vendor causing disruption. Different object and mechanism (cost vs service-disruption risk).
Benefit/wage costs = labor expense rising with minimum wage and benefits; retention = rate of staff staying (a productivity/turnover metric). Different metric and mechanism.
Case counts = level of COVID infections affecting demand; restrictions = government-imposed containment/dining limits. Different object (infection levels vs regulatory mandates) and mechanism.

Anti-American boycott = specific geopolitical demand risk targeting American brands; brand reputation = the broad asset of brand value and general reputational harm. Different scope (one specific cause vs the general asset) and mechanism.

Both concern CAKE product-recall risk and overlapping recall mechanism, but scope differs: 'bakery_product_recall' is explicitly narrowed to the bakery-division products and the cost of conducting a recall, while 'product_recall' is the company-wide recall/withdrawal monitoring process across restaurants. Same scope is not clearly established, so not a confident merge.

Market share = taking share within existing competitive markets via quality/traffic; new market entry = geographic expansion into new markets. Different object and mechanism (competitive share vs geographic growth).

Different metric. (a) = menu price increases at North Italia (a pricing action/lever). (b) = North Italia segment margins (a profitability outcome). Same brand scope but different object and different mechanism: a price hike is an input, a segment margin is a result. Fails same-object and same-mechanism.

Different object. (a) = balance-sheet operating lease liabilities (a dollar amount/financial obligation). (b) = the qualitative leasing environment / lease-term conditions for new sites. One is a recorded liability metric, the other is market leasing conditions. Different object and mechanism.

Different metric. (a) = pace/count of new restaurant openings and unit-growth development (CapEx, permitting, openings). (b) = sales performance/productivity of those newly opened units. Building units vs. how much the new units sell are different objects and mechanisms, even though related.

Different metric. (a) = capturing market share vs. the broader casual dining industry (relative share). (b) = traffic (guest counts) vs. the casual dining industry. Traffic is one input that can demonstrate share gains, but share and traffic are distinct objects measured differently. Fails same-object.

Same metric type (restaurant-level / four-wall margin) and same mechanism, but DIFFERENT scope. (a) is specifically the North Italia concept's margin; (b) is the Cheesecake Factory concept / portfolio restaurant-level margin (all evidence cites 'The Cheesecake Factory concept'). Different brand scope means keep separate.

Different object. (a) = integration of the Flower Child acquisition into operations (an integration process). (b) = Flower Child unit count and unit-growth potential (location footprint/expansion). Same brand but different object and mechanism.

Different object. (a) = customers' willingness to pay third-party delivery fees (a sales/demand risk tied to delivery pricing). (b) = operational risk of a third-party vendor failing to provide services (data loss, disruption). Entirely different mechanisms and objects despite shared 'third party' tokens.

Both evidence quotes are word-for-word identical: "pandemics and related containment measures, including the potential for quarantines or restriction on in-person dining" (CAKE 8-K, 2024 and 2025 filings — same boilerplate risk-factor language repeated year over year). Same object/metric: restriction on in-person dining. Same scope: CAKE, driven by pandemic containment measures. Same mechanism: quarantines/restrictions limiting in-person dining capacity. The two driver names only differ in framing (in_person vs pandemic) but describe the exact same risk. All three SAME tests pass. Add reversible SAME_AS.

Related but different object. (a) = minimum wage increases (broad labor-cost driver, often paired with benefit costs). (b) = tip credit wage specifically (the sub-minimum wage for tipped workers, and its potential elimination). The filings consistently name them as two distinct items ('minimum wage and tip credit wage'). Different object; keep separate.

Completely different objects. (a) = cash-on-cash returns on new unit investments (a return/ROI metric, 20-25% target). (b) = on-premise (dine-in) traffic as a share of total. Only the token 'on' overlaps. No shared object, scope, or mechanism.
Different object/metric: average check is dollars-spent-per-customer-per-visit; segment margin is a profitability percentage. Same scope (North Italia) but different object and mechanism.
Different metric: restaurant-level/four-wall margin is a profitability percentage; same-store (comparable) sales is a revenue-growth percentage. Different object and mechanism.

Both are weather disruptions to traffic (same mechanism), but scope differs: severe_weather evidence is generic catastrophic events (LA fires, 'unprecedented weather'); winter_weather is specifically cold/January weather. Object overlap is partial, not clearly the same.
Different object: restaurant_count is the number of company-owned units; traffic is the customer-visit growth/decline percentage. Distinct objects and mechanisms.

Different object/mechanism: brand_awareness is how well-known the brand is (drives faster sales ramp in new markets); brand_reputation is the brand's value/standing and the risk of negative publicity harming it. Distinct concepts despite shared 'brand' token.
Different object: margin is a profitability percentage; unit_count is the number of locations / unit-growth count. Distinct objects and mechanisms.

Both are margin guidance but different metric and scope: four-wall margin is restaurant/unit-level operating margin; net income margin is bottom-line company-wide net margin. Different object and scope.

Convertible notes are a subtype of debt, but debt_issuance evidence is a different, later event (2026 8-K Item 2.03, generic financial obligation) with broader scope, not clearly the same $575M convertible-notes object. Subset/superset relation, not a clear same-object match.
Different scope and metric: lunch_daypart_sales covers all lunch-daypart sales; midweek_lunch_traffic is specifically midweek traffic counts. Narrower scope plus sales-vs-traffic metric difference.

Entirely different financial concepts despite shared 'leverage' token: debt_leverage is debt-to-equity ratio / indebtedness; sales_leverage is operating leverage of sales over the cost base. Different object and mechanism.

a = bringing Flower Child brand under CAKE's umbrella (corporate acquisition/integration); b = Flower Child's app-based loyalty points rewards program. Different objects (M&A vs customer loyalty program); fails same-object.
Both are restaurant unit counts but different scope: international-licensed restaurants vs North Italia company-owned restaurants. Different populations of restaurants; fails same-scope.

Object/metric: both name weather (rain, snow, storms, fires, holidays-adjacent cold snaps) as the factor moving the company. The "adverse_weather" evidence quotes are literally about adverse weather conditions and natural disasters hurting customer traffic; the "weather" evidence quotes ("there was some weather impact... about 40 to 50 basis points", "the weather impacts have been very, very clear to us", "affected by the LA fires and the weather impact", "inclement weather") describe the exact same thing — weather hurting restaurant traffic/sales. Scope: identical — both are CAKE (The Cheesecake Factory), restaurant operations, customer traffic and same-store sales. Mechanism: identical — weather reduces customer visits and staffing, lowering sales. The "weather" bucket even lists same_as_variants ["weather_conditions","weather_disruption"] and its first quote is "inclement weather," while "adverse_weather" lists same_as_variants ["inclement_weather"] — so both already point at the inclement/adverse-weather concept. The plain word "weather" here is not a neutral/positive metric; in context every quote is about weather as a downside risk to traffic. The one near-positive quote ("a little bit of talk... about some positive weather") is still the same underlying object (weather affecting traffic), just the favorable direction of the same factor — direction does not split the driver. All three tests (same object, same scope, same mechanism) hold. Approve SAME with a reversible SAME_AS so the broader "weather" name absorbs the narrower "adverse_weather".

a = fair-value remeasurement of contingent consideration & compensation liability (a specific earnout-liability metric); b = general FRC acquisition-related expenses. Different object/mechanism (specific liability remeasurement vs broad acquisition costs); fails same-object.
a = AUV, absolute average sales per unit (dollar level per location); b = comparable/same-store sales, a percent change vs prior year. Different metrics; fails same-metric.
a = developing/growing/operating North Italia (unit expansion/growth risk); b = North Italia segment profit margins. Different objects (expansion vs profitability); fails same-object.
a = average weekly sales metric ($85,200); b = post-acquisition integration process of Flower Child. Different objects (sales metric vs integration activity); fails same-object.
a = creation of a direct financial obligation (debt); b = issuance of common stock (equity). Different financial instruments; fails same-object.

a = forward total-company CapEx guidance ($180-200M); b = historical actual capital expenditures for new restaurants only. Different scope (total vs new-restaurant) and nature (forward guidance vs reported actuals); fails same-scope.
a = market share in the casual dining industry; b = equity/stock-market price volatility. Unrelated objects sharing only the token 'market'; fails same-object.

Object/metric: both name the issuance (or perceived issuance/sale) of the company's common stock, or rights to acquire common stock — identical object. Scope: both are the same CAKE risk-factor language about issuing/selling its own common stock to the market — same scope. Mechanism: both describe the exact same causal path — new issuance or sale dilutes/depresses (declines) the trading price of the common stock. The two quotes are near-verbatim ("trading price of our common stock may significantly decline" vs "could depress the trading price of our common stock"). equity_issuance (with variant common_stock_issuance) and share_issuance (with variant share_issuance_dilution) are the same dilution driver under different names. All three tests pass → reversible SAME_AS.

a = execution risk of developing/growing/operating the North Italia concept; b = a KPI counting North Italia company-owned restaurants. Different object (strategy execution vs a headcount metric) and different mechanism.

a = reported comparable (same-store) sales for the North Italia brand specifically; b = forward-looking comp/same-store-sales guidance for the overall company. Different scope (one brand vs whole company) and different mechanism (reported actuals vs forward guidance).
Same metric (AUV) but different scope: a is Cheesecake Factory / general AUV (~$12M), b is Flower Child specifically (~$4.4M). Different brand scope fails the same-scope test.

Both drivers describe the identical competitive-pressure risk for CAKE (The Cheesecake Factory). The evidence quotes are nearly word-for-word the same across filings: "We compete directly and indirectly for customer traffic with national and regional casual dining/full-service dining restaurant chains, as well as independently-owned restaurants." Checking the three SAME tests: (1) Same object/metric — both track competition for customer traffic in the restaurant industry. (2) Same scope — both cover CAKE competing against the same set of rivals (national/regional dining chains + independent restaurants); "casual dining" vs "restaurant" is a labeling/wording difference, not a scope difference, and CAKE's own filings shifted the wording from "casual dining" to "full-service dining" over time while describing the exact same competitive set. (3) Same mechanism — competitive pressure on customer traffic, menu/food quality, service, location, and value. The only non-overlapping quote (8-K "more challenging and competitive environment") is consistent with the same competition theme. "casual_dining_competition" is a narrower-named subset of "restaurant_competition" but the evidence shows they refer to the same competitive dynamic for the same single company, so they are duplicates and warrant a reversible SAME_AS merge.
a = supply chain cost savings (a cost level); b = supply chain integration (operational integration improvements). Different object and different mechanism despite the shared 'supply chain' tokens.
a = operational integration of the Flower Child concept into CAKE; b = Flower Child's app-based loyalty/rewards points program. Different objects entirely; only the brand name overlaps.

a = broad self-insurance costs driven by insurance-market conditions/availability/regulation; b = the narrower workers' compensation insurance line item driven by claim activity. Different scope (broad category vs specific line) and different mechanism.

Both drivers describe the same object/metric: CAKE's program of building and opening new restaurant locations (units). Side A ("new_unit_development") evidence is dominated by "open as many as X new restaurants" guidance, unit-growth %, new restaurant openings driving revenue, and CapEx "for new unit development." Side B ("unit_development") evidence is the identical concept — CapEx "to support... unit development," "open as many as 25 new restaurants," "accelerate development with as many as 26 new restaurant openings," and "target that 25 openings this year." All three checks pass: (1) same object = pace/count of new restaurant unit openings; (2) same scope = CAKE company-wide across its portfolio of concepts; (3) same mechanism = capital-funded building and opening of new physical units driving revenue/unit growth. The "new_" prefix is cosmetic — B's own quotes say "new restaurants," and A already lists same_as_variants like new_restaurant_development. Reversible SAME_AS is safe.

a = demand for CAKE's branded retail/CPG (grocery) products; b = consumer demand for experiential dining (going out to eat). Different object and different mechanism; only the 'demand' token overlaps.

Different metric. (a) new_restaurant_unit_volumes = sales/revenue PER NEW UNIT (AUV of newly opened restaurants vs system) — its quote is about new units reaching 'the same unit volumes as the rest of the North system'. (b) new_unit_development = the COUNT/pace of opening new restaurants (20-22 openings, CapEx for development). Object differs: per-unit dollar productivity vs number/pace of openings. Fails same-object.

Different metric. (a) guest_satisfaction = overall satisfaction/CSAT/NPS scores. (b) guest_value_scores = perceived VALUE-for-money scores ('value scores are pretty stable', variant guest_value_perception). Value perception is a distinct survey dimension from overall satisfaction — a guest can be satisfied yet rate value low. Fails same-object.

Completely different concepts that only share the token 'legal'. (a) legal_fees = G&A legal expense line (cost driver). (b) legal_immigration = labor-supply risk from reduced levels of legal immigration shrinking the staffing pool. Different object, scope, and mechanism.

Related but distinct alcohol metrics. (a) alcohol_attach_rate = share of guests/checks that include an alcoholic beverage (attach/incidence). (b) alcohol_sales_mix = alcohol's proportion of total sales and trade-down within the category (mix). Attach rate (do they buy alcohol) and sales mix (what fraction of revenue is alcohol / trade-down) are different metrics. Fails same-object/mechanism.

Different object for the same brand. (a) north_italia_development = developing/growing/operating the North Italia concept (expansion risk). (b) north_italia_food_cost = lowering food costs at North Italia (cost-of-sales driver). Growth/expansion vs cost margin are different metrics and mechanisms.

Different metric for the same brand. (a) flower_child_off_premise_mix = share of sales that are off-premise (channel mix, 55%). (b) flower_child_same_store_sales = comparable-sales growth %. Channel mix vs comp-sales growth are different objects. Fails same-object.

Different object. (a) acquisition_expenses = the dollar COSTS/expenses booked for an acquisition (FRC acquisition-related expenses, a P&L cost driver). (b) acquisition_integration = operational RISK/complexity of combining businesses, employees, cultures, systems. A cost line vs an integration-execution risk are different metrics and mechanisms.

Actual vs forward-looking guidance of the same underlying metric. (a) same_store_sales = reported/historical comparable restaurant sales. (b) same_store_sales_guidance = forward EXPECTATIONS/assumptions for comp ('do you expect to be in that 1% to 2% range', 'comp assumptions'). Actual results and guidance are intentionally kept separate (the catalog distinguishes a metric from its guidance). Different scope (realized vs projected). Fails same-scope.
Two unrelated P&L line items sharing only the token 'expense'. Depreciation (non-cash asset write-down) vs interest expense (cost of debt/Revolver). Different object and mechanism entirely.

Both describe CAKE's customer base by income and its spending stability/resilience, and the quotes overlap heavily (high-income $100k+ cohort spending patterns staying stable/resilient). But (a) is framed specifically around HIGH-income consumers and trade-down benefit, while (b) is the broader 'cohort distribution and spending patterns' across income levels. Could be the same demand driver or a narrower vs broader cut — not confidently same scope on this thin evidence, so keep separate.

A = board raising the buyback program size (a capital-return decision, e.g. authorization raised to 66M shares). B = the IRA's 1% excise tax that increases the cost of repurchasing. Different object (authorization size vs tax cost) and different mechanism.

A = a sales-per-restaurant metric (AUV ~$7.7-8.0M). B = a risk/initiative about effectively developing and growing the North Italia concept. Different object (revenue metric vs growth-execution risk).

Same program (Cheesecake Rewards) but different object: A = the program itself as a sales-driving initiative (launch, design, offers). B = the measured level of member activity/engagement (a performance read on member behavior). Metric vs initiative differ.

Both are forward margin guidance for restaurant operations and 'four-wall margin' and 'restaurant-level margin' are often used interchangeably. But 'four-wall' specifically excludes some allocated costs, so it may be a subset rather than identical to restaurant margin. Cannot confirm same exact metric from evidence.
A = Flower Child profit margin (mid-teens target). B = Flower Child off-premise sales mix (55%, a sales-channel metric). Different object (profitability vs channel mix).

A = the broad industry demand/spending environment (softer environment). B = industry traffic trends (guest visits, BlackBox/NAPTRAC). Traffic is one input to demand but a distinct metric; different object.

A = total company revenue forecast (e.g. ~$3.6B for the year). B = a specific royalty revenue stream from CPG/international licensing. Different object (total revenue outlook vs one revenue line item).

A = new restaurant profit margin (ramping margins to maturity). B = new restaurant unit volumes (sales/AUV levels). Different object (profitability vs sales volume), even though both concern new units.

A = the broad COVID-19 pandemic impact (disruption, staffing absenteeism, slow-recovery areas). B = the specific mechanism of pandemic-related restrictions on in-person dining / quarantines. B is a narrower scope and mechanism, not the same driver.

A = governmental delays in licensing/permitting for new restaurant construction. B = federal government shutdowns affecting consumer confidence/spending and trade. Different objects entirely; only the word 'government' overlaps.

Different metrics. eps_surprise = earnings per share (a per-share dollar amount) beating/missing expectations. net_income_margin_surprise = net income margin (a percentage = net income / revenue) beating expectations. EPS is a per-share figure; margin is a profitability ratio. Different object/metric, so DIFFERENT.

Different objects. intangible_asset_impairment = write-down of indefinite-lived intangible assets (e.g. trade names/goodwill-like, $180.8M in fiscal 2020). long_lived_asset_impairment = write-down of physical long-lived assets (specific restaurant locations). Intangibles vs tangible/long-lived assets are distinct accounting categories. DIFFERENT.

Completely different concepts sharing only the word 'beverage'. alcoholic_beverage_licensing = regulatory/compliance risk for liquor licenses. food_beverage_cogs = cost of goods sold (food and beverage input costs as % of revenue). One is a regulatory driver, the other a cost-of-sales metric. DIFFERENT.

Different metrics for the same brand. flower_child_average_weekly_sales = a per-unit-per-week sales rate (e.g. $85,200 AWS). flower_child_sales = total/aggregate Flower Child revenue for a period (e.g. $127.5M for fiscal 2023). Average weekly sales (a unit-economics rate) vs total sales (an aggregate dollar amount) are distinct metrics. DIFFERENT.

Different scope. adverse_weather = a general/company-wide risk-factor concept (any adverse weather, natural disasters affecting traffic/staffing). california_weather = a specific event tied to one geography (record rainfall in California, the largest market). General risk factor vs a specific localized weather event differ in scope. DIFFERENT.

Different scope. north_italia_restaurant_level_margin = restaurant-level (four-wall) margin specifically for the North Italia brand (13.3%, 15% for mature locations). restaurant_margin_guidance = forward-looking guidance for overall/corporate restaurant margin (e.g. '15% level for the overall corporate', expected bps of expansion). Brand-specific actual margin vs company-wide forward guidance differ in scope. DIFFERENT.

Unrelated concepts sharing only 'market'. equity_market_volatility = stock price/volume fluctuations risk factor (capital-markets). market_share = the company's competitive share of casual dining sales/traffic. Capital-markets risk vs competitive market share are entirely different objects. DIFFERENT.

Unrelated concepts sharing only the word 'credit'. fica_tip_tax_credit = a payroll tax credit (FICA tip credit / SITCA program) reducing taxes. revolving_credit_availability = available borrowing capacity on a revolving credit facility (a financing/liquidity item). Tax credit vs credit facility availability are entirely different. DIFFERENT.

Different compensation types. incentive_compensation = cash bonus/incentive pay tied to performance (incentive comp paid, restaurant-level incentive comp expense). stock_based_compensation = equity-based comp expense (stock awards, true-ups). Cash incentive pay vs equity compensation are distinct expense categories. DIFFERENT.

Different object. 'alcoholic_beverage_licensing' = regulatory permits/licenses to serve alcohol (compliance/legal). 'food_beverage_costs' = COGS line item, the cost of food and beverage as a percent of sales. Token overlap on 'beverage' only; no shared object, scope, or mechanism.

Different object. 'legal_fees' = G&A legal expense affecting margins. 'third_party_delivery_fees' = fees customers pay to delivery platforms affecting comparable sales. Token overlap on 'fees' only; unrelated mechanisms and scopes.

Different scope. 'new_restaurant_margin' = margin of newly opened restaurants ramping to maturity (subset/early-stage units). 'restaurant_level_margin' = overall four-wall/restaurant-level margin for the portfolio/concept. Related metric type but distinct scope (new units vs all units); keep separate.

Different object/scope. 'operating_margin' = company-wide operating margin (e.g. 5.2% range, after G&A). 'restaurant_margin' = restaurant-level/four-wall margin (e.g. 15% range, before corporate G&A). These are distinct P&L levels even though both are forward guidance.

Different object/mechanism. 'convertible_note_conversion' = the mechanics/timing of noteholders converting notes (when conversion is permitted). 'convertible_note_dilution' = the dilutive impact on shares/EPS from conversion. Cause vs effect; distinct mechanisms; keep separate.

Different object. Menu pricing = price increases taken at North Italia (an input/lever). Same-store/comparable sales = the comp sales metric (an outcome). Token overlap on brand 'north italia' only; pricing is one driver of comps, not the same metric.

Completely different object. 'credit_facility_borrowings' = debt drawn under the Revolver Facility. 'fica_tip_tax_credit' = FICA tip tax credits / SITCA program tax benefit. Token overlap on 'credit' is coincidental (credit facility vs tax credit); unrelated.

Different scope/object. 'covid_pandemic' = the broad COVID-19 pandemic and its overall business disruption. 'pandemic_dining_restrictions' = the specific containment measure of in-person dining restrictions/quarantines (a narrower risk factor). One is a broad event, the other a specific mechanism; keep separate.

Different object. 'guest_rewards_program' = the Cheesecake Rewards loyalty program (marketing initiative). 'guest_satisfaction' = guest satisfaction/CSAT/NPS scores (a measured experience metric). Token overlap on 'guest' only; distinct concepts.

Same metric (capital expenditure) but different scope/mechanism: 'capex_guidance' is the forward-looking anticipated CapEx range provided as guidance, while 'capex' here is also forward-anticipated language but is intended as the actual/general capex driver distinct from the guidance bucket. The suggester run already maintains a separate guidance variant for many metrics (e.g. margin); since the actual-vs-guidance split is an intentional dimension in this catalog, merging would collapse that distinction. Keep separate (UNCLEAR-leaning to DIFFERENT).

Different metrics. 'development_guidance' is about new restaurant openings/unit growth count (open 26 new restaurants). 'margin_guidance' is about restaurant-level profit margin percentage (15% margin, bps expansion). Different object and mechanism despite shared 'guidance/restaurant' tokens.

Different objects. 'operations' = operating standards/control risk (licensees may not adhere to our operating standards). 'profitability' = licensee profit margins under commodity price pressure. Different mechanism (operational control vs cost/profit) despite same international-licensee scope.

Different objects. 'acquisition_expenses' = accounting line for FRC acquisition-related costs/expenses. 'flower_child_acquisition' = the strategic event of bringing the Flower Child brand under the umbrella (a specific deal/concept), not a cost metric. Different object and mechanism.

Entirely different objects sharing only the word 'market'. 'equity_market_volatility' = stock price/volume fluctuations. 'labor_market' = employment/wage conditions affecting margins. No shared object, scope, or mechanism.

Same brand scope (Flower Child) but different objects. AWS = a sales-volume KPI ($85,200/week). rewards_program = a loyalty app/points program. Different metric and mechanism despite shared brand tokens.

Same metric type (restaurant count KPI) but different scope. One counts company-owned units; the other counts international-licensed units. Mutually exclusive populations — distinct KPIs, not duplicates.

Different objects. eps_surprise = earnings/EPS vs expectations (net income per share). revenue_surprise = total revenue vs expectations (top line). Both are 'surprise-vs-guidance' but measure different line items — distinct metrics.

Different objects sharing only 'demand'. experiential_dining_demand = consumer demand for the dining experience. landlord_demand = landlords wanting CAKE concepts in their projects (real-estate/site demand). Different actor (consumers vs landlords) and mechanism.

Both quote the identical 10-K sentence about the bakery division producing for restaurants, licensees AND third-party customers. 'bakery_sales' is broader (whole bakery division across all channels); 'third_party_bakery_sales' is the narrower third-party channel subset. Scope differs (whole vs subset), but evidence is the same sentence and doesn't cleanly distinguish them — narrower is a subset of broader, so a SAME merge would over-merge. Not confidently same; flag as unclear.

Different objects. marketing_costs = P&L expense line (higher marketing costs from rewards program launch). social_media_marketing = competitive use of social media channels for messaging/differentiation. Different metric (cost line vs marketing channel/strategy) and mechanism.

Different object. lease_impairment = impairment of operating-lease right-of-use assets (specific urban-location lease ROU writedowns post-COVID). long_lived_asset_impairment = impairment of long-lived (property/equipment) assets at specific restaurant locations. CAKE's own 10-K disclosure 'impairment of assets and lease terminations expense' treats these as distinct components; lease impairment is a sub-item, not the same metric as long-lived asset impairment. Object differs, so not SAME.

Different object/metric. capex = the dollar level of capital expenditure ($190-200M to fund unit development). capex_inflation = the rate of cost increase in capital spending ('we have seen CapEx inflation'). One is a spending amount, the other is a price-change rate affecting that spending. Distinct metrics with distinct mechanisms.

Completely different objects and mechanisms. wage_hour_litigation = legal/litigation risk from wage-and-hour lawsuits (especially California). wage_inflation = the cost driver of rising labor wage rates vs menu pricing. Only the token 'wage' overlaps; one is a legal-exposure driver, the other is a labor-cost-margin driver.

Different scope. loyalty_program = the overall Cheesecake Rewards loyalty/rewards program as a whole (national launch, marketing program). loyalty_program_offers = a specific tactic WITHIN the program — the unpublished/personalized individualized offers used to drive incremental visits. The offers are a component/sub-feature of the broader program, not the same object. A second blind judge could reasonably keep these separate, so not a safe reversible SAME.

Different scope/mechanism in CAKE's usage. foreign_exchange_rate evidence = general FX rate fluctuations affecting foreign exchange/tariffs/cross-border taxation. us_dollar_exchange_rate evidence = specifically the value of the U.S. dollar relative to other currencies affecting cost/availability of internationally purchased goods (commodity sourcing). They reference different exposures (broad FX vs. USD-driven import cost). Not clearly the same metric; keep separate.

Different object/scope. flower_child_rewards_program = the rewards program for the Flower Child brand (app-based points program). guest_rewards_program = the Cheesecake Factory guest rewards program (national pilot/rollout, the Cheesecake Rewards). These are two distinct brands' loyalty programs (Flower Child vs Cheesecake Factory). Same_as for the guest one is 'cheesecake_rewards_program', confirming it is the Cheesecake brand, not Flower Child.

Different metric. Net income margin = bottom-line margin after all costs/taxes (guidance ~4-5%). Operating margin = operating-level margin (target ~5.2% range, before interest/taxes). These are distinct line-item margins at different points in the income statement, even though both are margin guidance. Different object, not SAME.

Different metric entirely. restaurant_count = number of North Italia company-owned restaurants (a unit count KPI). segment_margin = the profit margin of the North Italia segment. Only the brand name 'North Italia' overlaps; one is a count, the other is a profitability ratio.

Different metric. average_weekly_sales = a sales-volume KPI ($85,200/week, up 6%). margin = restaurant-level profitability target (mid-teens %). Only the brand 'Flower Child' overlaps; one is a revenue/volume measure, the other a profitability ratio.

Different object/mechanism. environmental_social_activism = activism (media campaigns, litigation) by advocates/opponents around environmental and social matters — an ESG/stakeholder pressure risk. social_media_publicity = reputational risk from negative publicity going viral on social media. Only the token 'social' overlaps; distinct risk drivers with distinct mechanisms.
Engagement measures member activity/satisfaction level; offers are the personalized/unpublished promotions used to drive incrementality. Different metric and mechanism within the same program.
Same metric (average weekly sales) but different brand/concept scope: Culinary Dropout (~$200K/wk) vs Flower Child (~$85K/wk). Scope differs.
Marketing campaigns is the broad set of promotional efforts; social media marketing is one specific channel (and appears as a competitive risk factor). Different scope/object.
New-restaurant ramp margin is concept-agnostic (bringing early-stage units up to speed); North Italia restaurant-level/four-wall margin is brand-specific. Different scope.

All three tests pass. Object/metric: both sides refer to the exact same thing — the COVID-19 pandemic. Every quote on the "pandemic" side explicitly names "the COVID-19 pandemic" (e.g., "The COVID-19 pandemic significantly disrupted our business", "the COVID-19 pandemic continued to affect our business"), and even the bare-"pandemic" quote ("areas which have not fully recovered from the pandemic") refers to the same COVID-19 event in context. The "covid_pandemic" side quotes are identical in subject (COVID-19's disruption of CAKE's business). Scope: same company (CAKE), same restaurant-operating-environment scope, same filings/period (both reference the 2024-02-26 10-K with literally overlapping quotes — both sides cite "the COVID-19 pandemic continued to affect our business during periods of accelerated case counts"). Mechanism: same — pandemic-driven business disruption (staff absenteeism, shifts in consumer behavior, suspended operations, slow recovery). "pandemic" here is just a shorter label for the same COVID-19 driver already captured by covid_pandemic (whose same_as_variants already includes covid19_pandemic). Merging via reversible SAME_AS is correct; "pandemic" is a generic synonym fully subsumed by the COVID-19 driver evidence.
Acquisition expenses are FRC deal-related costs; other operating expenses is a general expense line whose leverage improved. Different object/cost category.

All three tests pass. The evidence quotes are word-for-word IDENTICAL across both sides — the same CAKE 10-K risk-factor sentence ("Changes in our tax provision or an increase in our tax liabilities... could materially adversely affect our financial performance"), just filed in different years (2023/2024/2025 under "tax_policy" vs 2026 under "corporate_tax_policy"). Same object/metric: the company's tax provision and tax liabilities. Same scope: CAKE corporate-level tax exposure from law/regulation changes and tax-audit/litigation determinations. Same mechanism: tax-law/interpretation changes raising tax liabilities, hurting financial performance. The only difference is the driver_name string ("corporate_" prefix) and filing year — the underlying driver is one and the same. Reversible SAME_AS is warranted.
Capex inflation is cost escalation on capital spending; new-restaurant capex is the dollar amount spent on new units. Different metric (price pressure vs spend amount).
Bakery production capacity is a third bakery facility build-out; dining capacity restrictions are COVID-era government mandates limiting seating. Different object entirely.

Object: both are about consumer demand for dining — overlapping but not identical. Scope: "dining_demand" (plus its variants consumer_demand, consumer_dining_demand) is broad/general demand for going out to eat and for CAKE's "high-quality, differentiated dining experiences." "experiential_dining_demand" is a narrower, specific subtype — demand for the OUTING AS AN EXPERIENCE (energy, environment, atmosphere), not just the food/dining act itself. Mechanism differs: general dining demand is driven by consumer appetite for the food/value/quality; experiential demand is driven by the desire for an event/atmosphere/social occasion ("be in an environment that has a lot of energy"). The experiential one is a specific demand driver that sits INSIDE the broader dining-demand bucket, so merging would over-generalize the narrower, distinct experiential mechanism. Token overlap (demand, dining) plus shared "consumer demand" phrasing is what triggered the suggestion, but scope and mechanism are not the same. Fails tests 2 (scope) and 3 (mechanism) → keep separate. Note these could plausibly link as parent/child via optional_links, but that is not a reversible SAME_AS.
Physical bakery plant closure/damage risk vs. borrowing capacity under a revolving credit line. Only the word 'facility' overlaps; different object and mechanism.
Dilution from issuing new common shares vs. broad stock-market price/volume swings unrelated to company performance. Different object and mechanism; only 'equity' overlaps.
Physical climate impacts (droughts, severe weather) on operations vs. government greenhouse-gas disclosure rules (California). Different object and mechanism.
Hurricanes/earthquakes/wildfires causing restaurant closures vs. natural gas commodity price. Only 'natural' overlaps; unrelated objects.

Goodwill and indefinite-lived intangible assets are distinct balance-sheet objects with distinct impairment events (intangible side cites a specific $180.8M indefinite-lived intangible charge). Though filings group them in one test, they are not the same object.
Same brand scope, but food cost is a single input cost while restaurant-level/four-wall margin is a full profitability metric. Different metrics.

Even sharing a quote, international royalty revenue is a subset (international only) while royalty_revenue is the broader category (international + CPG). Different scope; one nests inside the other rather than being the same metric.
Same brand, but average weekly sales (dollars per unit) vs. number of locations (unit count). Different metrics.
Same brand, but average check (spend per customer) vs. traffic (number of customers/visits). Different metrics.
Workforce/labor shortfalls vs. supply-chain material shortages and transportation delays. Different object; only 'shortage' overlaps.

Different object/mechanism. lease_impairment = writing down the carrying value of right-of-use lease assets that lost value (e.g. underperforming urban locations post-COVID). lease_termination = exiting/ending a lease, which here generated termination INCOME/gains ($2.5M net income, $3.5M income). One is an asset write-down (loss); the other is closing out a lease (a gain event). Distinct accounting events.

Different scope. construction_labor_availability = availability of qualified tradespeople/construction workers to BUILD new restaurants (affects unit development/CapEx). labor_availability = restaurant/bakery staff to OPERATE the business (attract/retain qualified personnel, scarcity of operating labor). Different workforce, different mechanism (construction pace vs. store staffing).

Both refer to CAKE's bakery division selling baked goods (cheesecakes) to customers OUTSIDE its own restaurants. Object/metric: revenue from bakery products sold to non-restaurant buyers. Scope: same bakery division, same external customer base — the 10-K explicitly defines "third-party bakery customers" as buyers of the bakery division's products beyond CAKE's own restaurants and international licensees, which is exactly what the transcripts report as "external bakery sales" (the dollar-figure line item, e.g. $21.5M, $17.2M per quarter). Mechanism: identical — bakery facilities produce baked products that are sold externally rather than consumed internally by CAKE restaurants. "External" and "third-party" are the same concept here (sales outside the company's own restaurant operations), and the suggester's same_as_variants (bakery_external_sales, bakery_third_party_sales) reinforce this is one driver under two phrasings. The transcript side is the recurring quantified metric; the 10-K side is the narrative definition of the same revenue stream. All three tests pass: same object, same scope, same mechanism.

Token overlap (dining, restrictions) suggested this pair, but the three tests fail. (1) Different object/metric: A = "capacity restrictions" (a limit on HOW MANY people can be seated, e.g. 50% capacity). B = "restriction on in-person dining" (whether dine-in is allowed AT ALL, vs takeout/delivery only). Capacity caps and outright dine-in bans are distinct levers. (2) Mechanism differs: A's quote frames it as "further government mandates... including capacity restrictions" tied to wage inflation/staffing — an operational-constraint driver. B's quote frames it as "pandemics and related containment measures, including quarantines or restriction on in-person dining" — a pandemic-containment driver. Different triggering mechanism (general gov mandates vs pandemic containment). (3) Scope: A is a 2023 10-Q risk; B is a 2025 8-K pandemic scenario. A capacity restriction can apply while in-person dining is still open; an in-person dining restriction can be total closure. Because at least one of object, mechanism, and scope is false, verdict is DIFFERENT — keep separate, no SAME_AS.

All three tests pass for the same company (CAKE).  1) Same object/metric: Both track new restaurant ("unit") openings/development. Side B's filings say "open as many as 25 new restaurants in fiscal 2025" and "26 in fiscal 2026"; Side A's transcripts say the identical "25 new restaurants in 2025" and "26 new restaurant openings." These are literally the same numbers describing the same metric — count of new unit openings (plus the CapEx that funds that unit development).  2) Same scope: Both are company-wide across CAKE's full concept portfolio (Cheesecake Factory, North Italia, Flower Child, FRC). The 8-K in Side B and the transcripts in Side A both roll up to the same total restaurant-opening guidance for the same fiscal years.  3) Same mechanism: Both describe management's forward guidance on the pace/number of new restaurant openings (unit growth). Direct cross-source corroboration: CAKE_2025-02-19 appears on BOTH sides (transcript "open as many as 25 new restaurants in 2025" on Side A; 8-K same-day "now expects to open as many as 25 new restaurants in fiscal 2025" on Side B) — the same event reported in two source types. The only difference is the "_guidance" suffix and the source channel (transcript vs filing), not the underlying object, scope, or mechanism.  The "_guidance" suffix is a naming variant, not a different driver — both sides ARE guidance about unit development. Reversible SAME_AS is warranted.

Unrelated. credit_facility_availability = borrowing capacity under the revolving credit (Revolver) facility (a financing/liquidity metric). tip_credit_wage = labor-law wage concept where tip credits offset minimum wage obligations (a labor-cost driver). Token overlap on 'credit' only; completely different objects and mechanisms.

Different scope/mechanism. california_weather = region-specific weather in CAKE's largest market (record California rainfall). winter_weather = seasonal cold-month weather (January) affecting traffic broadly. One is geographic (California), the other is seasonal (winter). They can overlap but are distinct drivers; not the same object/scope.

Different metrics. menu_mix = the sales/product mix shift at North Italia ('mix was negative 2%'). restaurant_level_margin = four-wall/restaurant-level profitability percentage (13.3%, 15%). Mix can influence margin, but they are distinct measures (revenue composition vs. profit margin). Different objects.

Different object/mechanism. staff_absenteeism = employees not showing up for work (pandemic-driven missed shifts). staff_retention = keeping/holding onto employees over time (attrition rates, industry-leading retention). Absence on a given day vs. long-term turnover are distinct workforce metrics.

Different scope. beef_cost = broad beef commodity cost ('higher beef costs'). ground_beef_cost = specifically ground beef, a distinct sub-category the company calls out separately as 'tricky' and hard to contract long-term. Ground beef is a subset commodity with its own pricing dynamics; the company itself distinguishes it. Subset vs. whole = different scope.
a = costs/income from terminating or exiting leases (a P&L item from closing locations); b = the ongoing leasing environment and deal terms when signing leases. Different object and mechanism.

a = customers redeeming gift cards (revenue recognition, liability draw-down); b = selling gift cards via third-party distributors (a sales/distribution channel). Opposite ends of the gift-card lifecycle, different mechanism.
Same metric type (restaurant unit count) but different scope: a = aggregate FRC portfolio (parent group), b = North Italia alone (one brand inside FRC). Scope differs.
a = food-ingredient regulation/labeling (trans fats, sodium, GMOs, gluten); b = pandemics/epidemics disrupting operations. Entirely different objects despite shared token 'health'.
a = physical bakery manufacturing capacity (building a third bakery plant); b = off-premise channel serving capacity in restaurants. Different object and mechanism.
Same brand (Flower Child) but a = bringing it fully under ownership (an M&A event); b = its off-premise sales mix (~55%, a sales metric). Different object/metric.

Both concern competitor price/promo pressure and overlap, but a (discounting = direct price cuts, variant casual_dining_discounting) is narrower than b (broader promotional/marketing activity). Mechanism not clearly identical; cannot confirm SAME on the evidence.
A = breakage revenue from unredeemed gift cards (driven by redemption-pattern change). B = gift card sales volume via third-party distributors. Different metric and mechanism.

A = North Italia restaurant-level / four-wall margin (unit economics). B = North Italia segment margin, a distinct accounting measure that allocates more overhead/G&A. Different metric, even though both concern North Italia margins.

Both sides are the same metric (cost to build CAKE's restaurant premises), same scope (a single restaurant company building new locations), and same mechanism (raw materials + labor + build-out costs driving construction spend). The "construction_cost" side's evidence is identical in substance to the "restaurant_construction_cost" side: one shares the EXACT same quote ("the availability and/or cost of raw materials and labor used in construction" — same 10-K text appearing in the 2024 filing on side A and the 2025 filing on side B), and the transcript quote about tariff impacts on "construction costs" is plainly about building restaurants. Side B just names the same thing more explicitly (e.g., "Total construction costs to build our restaurant premises"). For a restaurant operator, generic "construction cost" IS restaurant construction cost — there is no separate non-restaurant construction object here. All three tests pass, so this is a reversible SAME_AS.

A = all COVID restrictions/containment measures (includes in-person dining restriction). B = government-mandated dining capacity restrictions, also in the COVID context. B looks like a subset of A rather than clearly the same object; A is a superset. Scope match is ambiguous, so not a confident SAME.

Same single company (CAKE / Cheesecake Factory), same metric (market share), same scope (casual dining), same mechanism (capturing share by outperforming the broader casual-dining peer set via comparable sales, traffic, and quality). (1) Object/metric: both track "market share." (2) Scope: Side A explicitly scopes every quote to "the broader casual dining industry"; Side B confirms the same scope by citing the "black box casual dining index" and is CAKE's own taking-share narrative — for Cheesecake Factory the relevant competitive set IS casual dining, so the generic "market_share" label and the scoped "casual_dining_market_share" label denote the identical concept. (3) Mechanism: both describe taking share by outperforming casual-dining peers (operator quality, sustained quality, comparable sales/traffic). The bare name "market_share" is just the less-specific label for the same company-specific driver — a classic missed duplicate where one record got the scoped name and one got the generic name. Add reversible SAME_AS.

A = general consumer disposable income as a macro condition affecting demand. B = spending behavior of the specific high-income ($100K+) consumer cohort. Different object and scope (broad macro factor vs a specific income segment).

Both name the same object: The Cheesecake Factory's (CAKE) cost of employee group medical/health coverage, reported as a component of labor. Scope is identical — company-wide workforce medical benefit cost embedded in labor margin. Mechanism is the same self-insured employee group-medical expense: side A's quotes tie it to "group medical cost due to larger claim activity," and side B's single transcript quote ("lower medical insurance expenses" within the Labor line) describes the same employee-medical line, just worded loosely; "pricing leverage" there refers to the broader labor movement, not a distinct premium-based metric. The naming variants explicitly cross-reference each other (side A lists group_medical_insurance_costs and health_insurance_cost; side B lists medical_insurance_expense), confirming "group medical" and "medical insurance" are the same line at this restaurant operator. All three tests (object, scope, mechanism) hold → reversible SAME_AS.

Different objects: G&A is corporate overhead (salaries, legal, admin) targeting a 6% of sales level; interest_expense is cost of debt (Revolver Facility, interest rate swap). Only the word 'expense' overlaps.

Different object and scope: one is the Flower Child brand's app-based points loyalty program itself; the other is launch cost of the namesake Cheesecake Factory Rewards program. A specific brand's program vs a cost line for a different brand's program.

Different objects: asset_impairment evidence is impairment of long-lived assets plus lease termination expense; goodwill_impairment is a distinct accounting item for goodwill/indefinite-lived intangibles tested annually. Only 'impairment' overlaps.
Same metric (AUV, ~$4.4M vs ~$7.7-8.0M) but different scope: Flower Child brand vs North Italia brand. Per-brand AUVs are distinct drivers.

Different object and scope: north_italia_menu_pricing is specific menu price increases at the North Italia brand; pricing_power is general pricing-capability commentary (California, namesake brand). Specific action vs general capability.

Same brand (Flower Child) but different objects: integration is the post-acquisition operational integration path; off_premise_mix is a 55% off-premise sales-channel metric. Only 'flower child' overlaps.

Different objects/mechanisms: legal_fees are recurring outside-counsel fees driving G&A basis-point changes; legal_settlement_costs are a discrete one-time settlement charge. Recurring expense line vs episodic settlement.

Different objects: competition is the competitive-threat risk factor (competing for traffic with chains/independents); market_share is an outperformance metric (capturing share vs the broader casual dining industry). Threat factor vs outcome metric.

Different objects: fica_tip_tax_credit is a federal income-tax credit tied to the SITCA program; tip_credit_wage is a labor-cost/minimum-wage concept (tip credit wage rate). Tax credit vs wage rate, opposite sides of the P&L. Only 'tip'/'credit' tokens overlap.
Same metric (comparable/same-store sales) but different scope: international geographies worldwide vs the North Italia domestic brand. Per-segment comps are distinct drivers.
Same brand (North Italia) but different metric: AUV is sales per location ($8.0M revenue), food cost is cost of goods. Different object and mechanism. Token overlap on 'north,italia' only.
Revolver/credit-facility debt drawdowns vs labor cost (tip credit wage). Completely different objects (financing vs wages). Pure 'credit' token collision.

Scope differs (the key failure): A = "casual dining," a segment of the restaurant industry (its evidence cites the "Black Box casual dining index" and "broader casual dining sales trends"); B = the whole "restaurant industry" ("the restaurant industry is navigating a softer environment"). Casual dining is a subset, not the same scope. Object also differs: A is a specific benchmarked sales index for one segment, B is a qualitative read on whole-industry demand/environment. Two of three tests fail, so per the rule the verdict is DIFFERENT. B's variant "restaurant_industry_sales" does not close the gap — still whole-industry, not casual-dining-only. Keep separate.

Same underlying metric (restaurant-level margin) but one is actual realized margin, the other is forward guidance/projection. Outcome vs expectation is a different driver; guidance side is explicitly forward-looking targets.
Margin (profitability %) surprise vs revenue (top-line $) surprise. Different object/metric despite both being 'vs expectations'. Pure 'surprise' token collision.
Same brand (Flower Child) but different object: M&A/ownership consolidation event vs weekly sales performance metric ($85,200). Different object and mechanism.

Both quotes describe supply-chain integration (Flower Child purchasing folded into Cheesecake supply chain). The 'efficiency' side's evidence is actually about integration, so the named distinction (cost gains vs combining systems) is not cleanly supported. Thin, ambiguous evidence; cannot confirm same object/mechanism.
Profit margin forecast (financial guidance) vs customer satisfaction metric (NPS). Completely different objects. Pure 'net' token collision.

Targeted daypart-specific messaging (driving a specific day part) vs general/social marketing campaigns. Different scope and mechanism; related theme but not the same driver. 'marketing' token overlap.
Same metric type (menu mix shift) but different scope: company/Cheesecake-concept-wide menu mix vs specifically North Italia's menu mix. Different concept/brand scope, so not the same driver.

FAST Act = California minimum-wage legislation for fast-food workers (a cost/labor driver). California weather = record rainfall affecting traffic/sales. Different object and mechanism; only shared token is 'california'.

Market share = capturing sales/traffic versus the broader casual dining industry (revenue/competitive driver). Labor market = stability of employment conditions affecting wages and margins (cost driver). Different object, scope, and mechanism.

Both concern the Cheesecake Rewards loyalty program, but engagement = activity/usage level of existing members, while enrollment = acquisition/sign-up of new members. Distinct metrics and mechanisms (member activity vs. membership growth). Evidence keeps them separable; enrollment side even has a quote distinguishing 'membership enrollment' from 'engaged in the program'.

New unit development = opening/building new restaurants (growth/capex driver). New unit sales transfer = cannibalization, where newly opened restaurants pull sales from existing ones (a negative comparable-sales effect). Different object and mechanism despite shared 'new/unit' tokens.

Site availability = availability of high-quality real estate sites for growth. Revolving credit availability = undrawn capacity on the credit facility (liquidity). Completely different objects; only shared token is 'availability'.

Gift card breakage = revenue recognized from unredeemed gift cards (a revenue item, $17.3M). Gift card inventory write-down = a non-recurring $7.9M cost charge for physical gift-card inventory. Different object (revenue vs. cost), opposite P&L direction, different mechanism.

Both about the North Italia brand, but restaurant-level margin = profitability metric (four-wall margin %), while traffic = guest count change. Different object and metric; scope (brand) overlaps but object/mechanism differ.

Operating lease liabilities = balance-sheet liability for lease obligations/extension options. Restaurant lease renewal = risk/cost of renewing leases on similar terms. Different object (recorded liability vs. renewal risk/cost) and mechanism; only shared token is 'lease'.

Consumer spending = broad macro consumer confidence/spending environment. Income cohort spending = the relative distribution and stability of spending across the company's own income-based customer cohorts. Different scope (macro vs. internal cohort mix) and object; only shared token is 'spending'.

Alcohol sales mix = share of sales from alcohol/alcoholic beverages (and trade-down within it). Bar sales mix = share of sales generated at the bar (a location/area of the restaurant, ~23%). Related but distinct objects and mechanisms; bar mix is a venue-area measure, alcohol mix is a product-category measure.

Borrowings = amount drawn/owed under the facility; availability = unused borrowing headroom. Opposite sides of the same facility, different metrics. Same object/scope fails on metric: outstanding balance vs remaining capacity.

consolidated_revenue is actual realized reported revenue ('increased 9.1%'); revenue_guidance is forward-looking forecast ('we anticipate total revenue...'). Different object: realized result vs projection. Mechanism differs (measured vs estimated).
branded_retail = CPG packaged-goods channel demand; dining_demand = in-restaurant consumer demand for dining experiences. Different scope/channel (retail product vs restaurant).

Menu pricing = price level taken on the menu; restaurant-level (four-wall) margin = unit profitability. Pricing is one driver of margin, not the same metric. Different object: price input vs profit output.
cash_on_cash = investment return on new-unit capital (20-25% target); on_premise_incident_rate = dining-room illness/incident frequency vs 2019. Wholly unrelated, only share token 'on'.

off_premise_mix = off-premise % of Flower Child sales (55%); off_premise_capacity = added throughput capacity from on-to-off shift. Different object: sales-mix ratio vs capacity. Different brand scope too (Flower Child vs general).
industry_sales = dollar sales trend (Black Box index); traffic = guest counts/foot traffic. Different metrics: sales $ vs traffic counts. Sales can move via price even when traffic falls.

international_licensing = overseas restaurant openings/expansion program under licensing agreements; licensing_royalties = the royalty/fee revenue stream earned from licensees' sales. Different object: expansion activity vs revenue line item.

integration = operational absorption of the acquired Flower Child brand ('continue down the integration path'); sales = the brand's revenue figures. Same brand scope but different object: operational process vs sales metric.
capex_guidance = forecast capital-spend amount ($180-200M); capex_inflation = cost-pressure inflation on construction/equipment. Different object: planned spend level vs cost-escalation driver.

food_beverage_costs is the reported P&L line 'Food and beverage costs' as % of revenue; food_costs evidence is 'actual-to-theoretical food cost inefficiencies' at new complex restaurants (an operational efficiency concept). Different object and mechanism.

delivery_cost = the company's cost to deliver ('delivery costs will significantly increase'); delivery_fees = consumers' willingness to pay third-party delivery fees affecting comparable sales. Company cost vs consumer-facing price/demand factor — different object and mechanism.

Token 'market' is coincidental: equity_market_volatility = stock-market price/volume fluctuations affecting share price; new_market_entry = entering new geographic restaurant markets. Unrelated objects.
Same brand but different metrics: acquisition/integration of Flower Child under the umbrella vs Flower Child per-restaurant average unit volume ($4.4M AUV). Different object and mechanism.

dividend_policy = strategic risk of failing to pay/grow a dividend (investor-confidence/stock-price risk); quarterly_dividend = a specific declared $0.30/share quarterly dividend amount. Policy/risk vs a concrete declared payout — different object and scope.

california_weather is scoped to California (record rainfall in largest market); severe_weather is general unprecedented weather including LA fires and holiday shifts. Different scope — one is a California-specific subset, the other is broad weather impact.
Same metric type (unit count) but different objects: Flower Child brand (38 locations, ~20% growth) vs the aggregate Other FRC portfolio (10-15% growth). Different brand/segment scope.

lease_impairment = accounting write-down of underperforming leased (post-COVID urban) locations; restaurant_lease_terms = the leasing/negotiation environment and terms for leases. Different object and mechanism.

tip_credit_wage = a specific regulatory mechanism (minimum/tip-credit wage rates and their elimination in certain states); wage_inflation = broad wage rate inflation as a cost-pressure metric. A specific legal wage floor vs general wage inflation — different object and mechanism.
Same brand (North Italia) but entirely different metrics: average check per customer (~$34.60 lunch/$44.40 dinner) vs company-owned restaurant count. Different object.

Development = building/opening new restaurants (unit count, capex, timing). Returns = financial payback/ROI on those units. Same brand and project, but different metric and mechanism (opening activity vs investment returns).

Both drivers measure the exact same object: CAKE's restaurant employee retention rate (the percentage of hourly staff and managers who stay vs leave). Three-part test all pass:  1. SAME OBJECT/METRIC: Both track "retention rates" of restaurant workers. The two evidence sets quote the SAME underlying phenomenon and even the SAME source documents/dates. Example: the "employee_retention" side quotes the 2025-04-30 transcript "manager and staff retention," while "staff_retention" quotes the parallel 8-K language "hourly staff and manager retention." Several 8-Ks use the identical boilerplate ("improvements in labor productivity, food efficiency, wage management and retention across both hourly staff and managers"). The same_as_variants are also mirror images: employee_attrition vs. staff_attrition.  2. SAME SCOPE: Both cover the same population — CAKE's hourly staff AND managers across its restaurants. Both sides repeatedly say "both hourly and at the manager levels" / "hourly staff and manager retention." No scope difference (neither is e.g. corporate-only or executive-only).  3. SAME MECHANISM: Both describe keeping employees on the job (low attrition) driving labor productivity, efficiency, wage management, and execution. Identical causal chain in both evidence sets.  "Employee" and "staff" are pure synonyms here — the company itself uses "staff," "people," and "employees" interchangeably for the same retention metric. This is a token-level naming split of one driver, so merge with a reversible SAME_AS.

'Facility' is a homonym here. One = revolving credit facility (debt/financing/borrowings). Other = physical restaurant facility-related operating costs. Different object entirely; token overlap is coincidental.

One = share of sales that are off-premise at Flower Child brand (a sales-mix metric). Other = marketing promotions that drive off-premise orders (a promotional activity). Different object and mechanism.

Recall = food-safety product withdrawal process. Sustainable product supply = shortages of legally-mandated eco/sustainable packaging products. 'Product' overlap is incidental; different object, scope, and mechanism.

Minimum wage = legally mandated wage floors (regulatory driver). Wage inflation = market-driven labor cost increases. Different mechanism (statutory mandate vs market pressure); CAKE's own filings treat them as distinct, related forces.
Capex = capital spending to build new restaurants. Unit volumes = sales/revenue per restaurant. Same brand/project context but different metric (investment outflow vs sales productivity).
One = availability of qualified tradespeople for construction. Other = credit available on the revolving credit facility. 'Availability' overlap is coincidental; different object and mechanism.
Margin = profitability of the North Italia segment. Traffic = guest/visit counts. Same brand but different metric (profitability vs demand volume).
Food cost = cost of ingredients at North Italia. Menu mix = shift in sales composition/mix. Same brand but different metric (input cost vs sales-mix shift).
Same brand scope (North Italia) but different objects: AUV is sales dollars per restaurant; menu_pricing is a price-increase lever. Different metric and mechanism.
Only the word 'compensation' overlaps. Incentive comp is bonus pay tied to net income; workers' comp insurance is cost of injury-claim insurance. Different object and mechanism.
Capex is capital dollars spent building new restaurants; food_cost_efficiency is the operating-margin ramp/inefficiency at new units. Different object (capital spend vs operating margin).
Same brand (Flower Child) but different metrics: average weekly sales in dollars vs off-premise percentage mix.
Different demand objects: branded_retail is consumer/CPG demand for packaged retail products; landlord_demand is landlords wanting the brand in their developments.

Shipping/transportation disruption is one channel within the broader supply chain; the b-side explicitly bundles transportation delays and 'disruptions in shipping', but supply_chain_disruption is strictly broader scope (commodities, permitting). Not cleanly same scope.
Different scope: consumer_spending is broad/all consumers; low_end_consumer_spending is specifically the low-end segment. Subset, not identical scope.
Only 'hedging' overlaps. Commodity hedging manages food-commodity cost risk; convertible_note hedging is investor hedging of the Notes affecting stock price. Different object and mechanism.
Same metric type (comparable sales) but different scope: North Italia brand-specific comps vs the broader/Cheesecake Factory concept comps. Different scope.

Object/metric differs: "efficiency" = doing the supply chain better/cheaper per unit (integrating Flower Child purchasing into one operation); "scale" = growing size/volume to get buying leverage (leveraging scalable supply chain, benefiting from increasing company scale). Mechanism differs: efficiency = consolidation/integration to cut waste; scale = volume-driven purchasing power. Scale CAN drive efficiency, but they are distinct levers — one is the size, the other is the per-unit performance. The quotes mix "scale" language on both sides, yet the named drivers separate efficiency from scale as the operative metric. Fails object/metric and mechanism tests, so not SAME. Keep separate.
a = Flower Child dollar sales/revenue ($127.5M etc.); b = number of locations / unit growth (38 locations, ~20% annual unit growth). Different object and metric (revenue vs store count).
a = average unit volume, dollar sales per location (~$8M/$7.7M AUV); b = traffic, the change in guest counts (negative 4%/6%). Different metric (dollar volume per unit vs customer count).
a = reduced legal immigration affecting labor supply; b = legal settlement / litigation expense. Unrelated concepts sharing only the token 'legal'.
a = operational integration of the Flower Child concept; b = Flower Child comparable/same-store sales metric. Different object (integration process vs sales metric).
a = consumer demand for CAKE's own differentiated dining experiences (company-specific); b = the broad restaurant industry environment/demand. Different scope (company vs whole industry).
a = production capacity / building a third bakery facility (capex/capacity); b = bakery production costs (higher production labor). Different metric (capacity/capex vs cost).
a = North Italia food cost (margin input); b = North Italia comparable/same-store sales (top-line metric). Different metric.

Scope differs. "weather" is the broad, all-seasons driver — its evidence spans heat, LA fires/Texas concentration, generic "watch the weather" pockets, and unspecified seasonal impacts across many quarters. "winter_weather" is the narrower seasonal subset, anchored to a single quote about "the weather in January" (cold-season disruption). Object (weather) and mechanism (traffic/sales impact) overlap, but the scope test fails: one is a general year-round driver, the other a specific winter/cold-season effect. Same-as variants on side A ("weather_conditions", "weather_disruption") confirm the broad node is meant to absorb general weather, not the winter-specific cut. Keep them separate; collapsing the seasonal node into the general one would lose the winter-specific signal. Note: the lone winter_weather quote ("weather in January") could arguably be folded, but the canonical names assert distinct scopes, so the safe call is DIFFERENT, not SAME.

a = noteholders exercising the right to convert the 2026 Notes; b = investor hedging activity that could depress the stock price. Different mechanism (conversion event vs hedging-driven price pressure).

a = regulatory licenses/permits required to serve alcohol; b = royalty revenue received from licensees based on their restaurant sales. Unrelated (compliance permits vs royalty revenue), sharing only 'licensing'.
Different brands: Flower Child location count vs North Italia restaurant count. Same metric type (store count) but different scope/object — two separate concepts under CAKE.
Same brand (Flower Child) but different metric: AUV is per-store revenue rate ($4.4M/unit), while sales is total brand revenue ($127.5M). Different object/scope.
Same brand but different metric: average check is revenue per customer ($34.60 lunch); food cost is an input cost. Different object and mechanism.
Same brand but different metric: development is unit growth/expansion risk; menu mix is sales mix percentage (negative 2%). Different object and scope.
Interest expense is a P&L dollar line item ($2.3M); interest rate is a macro condition/driver. Different object — one is the cost incurred, the other the external rate environment.
Bakery sales to third-party wholesale customers vs third-party food delivery providers (DoorDash). Entirely different object — wholesale baked-goods revenue vs delivery channel/risk.
Same context (new restaurant ramp) but different metric: food cost efficiency is one input component; margin is the overall profitability outcome. Different scope — component vs aggregate.
Same brand but different scope: average weekly sales is one specific metric ($200k/week); unit economics is a broad concept covering full profitability profile. Specific metric vs broad set.
Distinct P&L line items: depreciation (D&A, ~$26M-$115M) vs G&A (targeting 6% of sales). Different object — separate expense categories.

Dividend is the actual cash distribution/payments ($13M/quarter); dividend policy is the strategy/risk of sustaining/raising it (risk-factor framing). Different object and mechanism — the payment vs the governance/risk.
Different object/metric: a is a count of North Italia units; b is restaurant-level (four-wall) margin percentage. Same brand/scope but different metric and mechanism.
a is the level/normalization of party size; b is the mix/distribution of party sizes and its per-capita-spend effect. Different object (level vs mix-shift) and mechanism.
a is food-safety recall risk/cost for bakery products; b is supply shortages of sustainable (packaging/materials) products. Different objects and mechanisms; only 'product' token shared.
a is comparable/same-store sales growth (a sales metric); b is number of Flower Child locations / unit growth (a count). Same brand, different metric.
a is consumer demand for dining experiences (end customers); b is landlord demand for CAKE concepts as tenants (real-estate counterparties). Different object and mechanism.
a is targeted messaging to drive specific dayparts; b is social-media channel marketing/competition. Different mechanism and channel.
Same metric (traffic) but different scope: a is industry/sector-wide traffic (BlackBox/NAPTRAC benchmark); b is CAKE's own restaurant customer traffic. Scope prong fails.
a is lease termination (ending leases, termination costs/income); b is lease renewal risk/cost (extending leases). Opposite lease events; different object and mechanism.
a is the rewards program overall and its sales-driving effect; b is the narrower enrollment/membership-growth metric (member acquisition). Different object (program-as-driver vs enrollment count).
a is impairment charges on long-lived assets (non-cash expense); b is long-term debt outstanding (balance-sheet liability). Only 'long' token shared; unrelated.

Different metrics. (a) is menu price increases at North Italia (e.g. 'price increase in October ... for North Italia', 'were you behind on pricing'). (b) is guest traffic counts ('Traffic was negative 4%'). Price taken vs. number of guests are distinct objects with distinct mechanisms, even though both are North Italia brand.

Different scope.
